message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T109 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T109    Base commit: 8d8fa77 (current main HEAD)
headline: Persistent wanbond0 TUN across daemon restarts (I7 code)
description: Per Q38 (belt-and-suspenders, code half): make wanbond0 survive daemon restarts so addresses/routes/rules referencing it are not dropped on every restart. Opt-in config key (e.g. top-level `tun_persist = true`, default false so existing teardown semantics are unchanged): on start, set TUNSETPERSIST (or adopt an already-existing persistent device by name); on Close, leave the persistent device in place (link stays, session state torn down). Document the interaction with D39/NM (a persistent device still needs the unmanaged-devices drop-in on NM hosts) in the key's reference entry. Beware the single-engine guard and reload paths (internal/device/device.go) — persistence must not break SIGHUP reload or the restart-on-failure supervisor flow.
acceptance: Netns e2e: with tun_persist=true, an address assigned to wanbond0 survives a full daemon stop/start (the D5/I7 production failure mode) and the interface keeps the SAME ifindex across the restart; the persistent device does not become NM-managed (documented invariant; asserted where the fixture permits). With the default false, behavior is unchanged (device disappears on Close, existing e2e suite green). Relates D39 in acceptance only. go test ./... and -tags e2e green.

IMPORTANT: the -tags e2e netns suite needs privileged hardware you CANNOT run here — write the e2e test so it COMPILES and vets (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` + `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e` must pass); note e2e execution deferred (G2 pattern). Validate the tun_persist=false default (unchanged teardown) and the config-key plumbing via runnable unit tests where possible.
NOTE: T100 already added device.Up IFF_UP handling (internal/device/{device.go,linkup_linux.go,linkup_other.go}) — read it; your TUNSETPERSIST logic composes with it. Use golang.org/x/sys/unix (TUNSETPERSIST ioctl); a !linux stub may be needed mirroring linkup_other.go.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Add the tun_persist config key to internal/config (top-level) and the reference doc entry (docs/install.md and/or wanbond.example.toml — sync docs with code per AGENTS.md).

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t109-fix implement/T109` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

1	// Package device brings a wanbond tunnel up from a validated configuration: it
2	// creates the TUN interface, drives the embedded amneziawg-go engine over the
3	// multipath Bind, and applies the WireGuard (and, when configured, amnezia
4	// obfuscation) parameters via the engine's UAPI. It owns ONLY the tunnel engine
5	// — interface addressing and routing are left to the operator (systemd/wg-quick
6	// style), so the daemon stays free of privileged shell-outs. The interface name
7	// is exposed via Tunnel.Name for that external configuration step.
8	package device
9	
10	import (
11		"context"
12		"crypto/rand"
13		"encoding/hex"
14		"fmt"
15		"reflect"
16		"strings"
17		"sync"
18		"time"
19	
20		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
21		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
22	
23		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
24		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
25		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
27		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
28		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
29		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
30		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
31	)
32	
33	// metricsShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the /metrics endpoint on
34	// Close (and on a reload that rebinds it). The endpoint is loopback-only with no
35	// long-lived scrapes, so this is comfortably generous.
36	const metricsShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
37	
38	// defaultTUNName is the requested interface name; the kernel honours it unless it
39	// collides (it never does across the edge and concentrator network namespaces).
40	const defaultTUNName = "wanbond0"
41	
42	// tunMTU is the tunnel (TUN) MTU for a config: the default path MTU minus the
43	// IP+UDP, outer DATA-frame, and WireGuard transport overheads, so a full-MTU inner
44	// packet never fragments on the wire (see internal/bind mtu.go and docs/p1-mtu.md).
45	// This is smaller than the plain-WireGuard 1420 because the bonding layer adds its
46	// own outer DATA frame per datagram; with FEC enabled it is a further
47	// bind.FECParityMTUPenalty smaller so a full-size PARITY frame also fits the path MTU
48	// (T24) rather than fragmenting.
49	func tunMTU(cfg *config.Config) int {
50		return bind.InnerMTU(bind.DefaultPathMTU, cfg.FEC.Enabled)
51	}
52	
53	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the
54	// multipath Bind. Close tears all three down.
55	type Tunnel struct {
56		dev  *awgdevice.Device
57		tun  tun.Device
58		name string
59		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
60		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
61		bind *bind.Multipath
62		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
63		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
64		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
65		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
66		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
67		cfg *config.Config
68		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
69		reloadMu sync.Mutex
70		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
71		log log.Logger
72		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
73		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
74		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
75		stopProbes func()
76		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
77		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
78		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
79		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
80		stopReconcile func()
81		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
82		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
83		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
84		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
85		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
86		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
87		stopHubFailover func()
88		// amnezia is the obfuscation profile this tunnel holds against the
89		// process-global amnezia guard (see amneziaGuard); Close releases it.
90		amnezia     config.Amnezia
91		releaseOnce sync.Once
92	
93		// metricsSrc is the live metrics.Source over the Bind; it is stable for the tunnel's
94		// life (the Bind pointer never changes), so a reload that rebinds the endpoint reuses
95		// the SAME Source — its derived-throughput last-sample state survives the rebind.
96		metricsSrc metrics.Source
97		// metricsSrv is the running /metrics endpoint, nil when [metrics].listen is empty.
98		// It is (re)assigned by applyMetricsLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so
99		// a SIGHUP-driven rebind never races shutdown. metricsListen mirrors the address it
100		// is bound to so a reload can detect a listen change without inspecting the server.
101		metricsSrv    *metrics.Server
102		metricsListen string
103	}
104	
105	// SINGLE-ENGINE-PER-PROCESS INVARIANT (defect D2).
106	//
107	// amneziawg-go stores the amnezia magic-header message types in PACKAGE-GLOBAL
108	// variables — device.MessageInitiationType, MessageResponseType,
109	// MessageCookieReplyType, MessageTransportType. (*device.Device).IpcSet assigns
110	// them (in handlePostConfig) from a configured engine's profile, and
111	// (*device.Device).Close restores them to the WireGuard defaults (in
112	// resetProtocol) UNCONDITIONALLY — closing ANY engine, plain or configured,
113	// reverts the process-global message types to their defaults.
114	//
115	// Two consequences make a CONFIGURED (amnezia) engine require PROCESS
116	// EXCLUSIVITY:
117	//   - a second configured engine would overwrite the first engine's message-type
118	//     framing at IpcSet; and even with the SAME profile, closing the first engine
119	//     runs resetProtocol and reverts the globals to defaults under the second,
120	//     still-live engine, silently dropping its tunnel to plain-WireGuard framing.
121	//   - closing ANY other engine — a PLAIN (unconfigured) one included — runs
122	//     resetProtocol and resets the globals out from under a live configured engine.
123	//
124	// So the rule wanbond ASSERTS (rather than vendor-patching the fork) is:
125	//   - a configured engine may come up only when NO other engine is live, and
126	//   - no engine (plain or configured) may come up while a configured engine is live.
127	//
128	// PLAIN engines may coexist with each other: they never set the globals, and
129	// resetProtocol on their Close only restores the defaults they already use, so it
130	// is idempotent among them. wanbond runs exactly one tunnel per process, so the
131	// guard only ever trips on genuine misuse.
132	type amneziaGuard struct {
133		mu         sync.Mutex
134		plainLive  int  // number of live plain-WireGuard (unconfigured) engines
135		configLive bool // whether a configured (amnezia) engine is live (at most one)
136	}
137	
138	// globalAmneziaGuard enforces the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant for
139	// every Tunnel brought up in this process.
140	var globalAmneziaGuard amneziaGuard
141	
142	// acquire registers an about-to-start engine against the process-exclusivity rule
143	// (see amneziaGuard). A configured (amnezia) engine is admitted only when NO other
144	// engine is live. A plain engine is admitted only when no configured engine is
145	// live; plain engines may coexist with one another. The caller must release the
146	// SAME profile exactly once when the engine is torn down (plain engines included —
147	// release is no longer a no-op for them).
148	func (g *amneziaGuard) acquire(a config.Amnezia) error {
149		g.mu.Lock()
150		defer g.mu.Unlock()
151		if a.Configured() {
152			if g.configLive || g.plainLive > 0 {
153				return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start an amnezia-configured engine while another engine is live: " +
154					"amneziawg-go keeps the magic-header message types in process-global state and resets them to defaults on ANY engine's Close, " +
155					"so a configured engine requires process exclusivity — at most one, with no other engine alongside it (D2)")
156			}
157			g.configLive = true
158			return nil
159		}
160		if g.configLive {
161			return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start a second engine while an amnezia-configured engine is live: " +
162				"closing this engine would reset amneziawg-go's process-global message types to defaults under the live amnezia tunnel (D2)")
163		}
164		g.plainLive++
165		return nil
166	}
167	
168	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
169	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
170	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
171		g.mu.Lock()
172		defer g.mu.Unlock()
173		if a.Configured() {
174			g.configLive = false
175			return
176		}
177		if g.plainLive > 0 {
178			g.plainLive--
179		}
180	}
181	
182	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
183	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
184	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
185	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
186	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
187		clg := lg.Component("device")
188	
189		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
190		if err != nil {
191			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
192		}
193		name, err := tunDev.Name()
194		if err != nil {
195			_ = tunDev.Close()
196			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
197		}
198	
199		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
200		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
201		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
202		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
203		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
204			_ = tunDev.Close()
205			return nil, err
206		}
207		ok := false
208		defer func() {
209			if !ok {
210				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
211			}
212		}()
213	
214		// One live *telemetry.Prober per path drives on-wire liveness. The SAME prober
215		// values back the scheduler's PathHealth AND the bind's probe transport, so the
216		// liveness the probe loop measures is exactly the liveness the scheduler selects
217		// on (T37 replaces the sched.AlwaysUp placeholder here).
218		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, clg)
219		if err != nil {
220			_ = tunDev.Close()
221			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
222		}
223		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
224		if err != nil {
225			_ = tunDev.Close()
226			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
227		}
228		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
229	
230		uapi, err := uapiConfig(cfg)
231		if err != nil {
232			dev.Close()
233			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build UAPI config: %w", err)
234		}
235		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi); err != nil {
236			dev.Close()
237			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: apply UAPI config: %w", err)
238		}
239		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
240			dev.Close()
241			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring up: %w", err)
242		}
243	
244		// The engine has opened the bind (dev.Up → BindUpdate → Open), so the per-path
245		// sockets exist: start the probe cadence now. Close stops it before dev.Close.
246		// The interval is the SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH telemetry default, which also arms
247		// the bind's receive-path liveness sweep throttle (D15).
248		stopProbes := mpBind.StartProbeLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
249		// Start the background deferred-path reconciler alongside probing (T55): a path the
250		// tolerant Open left DOWN (its source_addr not yet assignable — a 5G modem without a
251		// lease at boot) is retried and promoted to a live path as its address appears,
252		// WITHOUT a tunnel restart. Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe loop.
253		stopReconcile := mpBind.StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)
254		// Start the edge-side hub-failover monitor (T57): on hub loss (every path to the
255		// active concentrator DOWN) it advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the
256		// bond's remote, and re-handshakes. A no-op on the concentrator, a single-concentrator
257		// edge, or a bind without the probe transport. Started AFTER dev.Up so the engine peer
258		// the re-handshake looks up exists (IpcSet added it above). Close stops it before
259		// dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
260		stopHubFailover := startHubFailover(cfg, mpBind, probers, dev, clg)
261	
262		t := &Tunnel{
263			dev: dev, tun: tunDev, name: name, bind: mpBind, cfg: cfg, log: clg,
264			stopProbes: stopProbes, stopReconcile: stopReconcile, stopHubFailover: stopHubFailover, amnezia: cfg.Amnezia,
265			// The Source reads live per-path counters/telemetry from the Bind and derives
266			// throughput from the byte-counter delta between scrapes (see metricsSource). It is
267			// built unconditionally (cheap) so a reload that later turns [metrics].listen ON has
268			// a Source ready; the endpoint itself is started only when a listen is configured.
269			metricsSrc: newMetricsSource(mpBind, telemetry.SystemClock{}),
270		}
271	
272		// Stand up the /metrics endpoint when configured. A non-loopback listen is refused
273		// by metrics.NewServer (fail fast) — surface it as an Up failure rather than booting
274		// a tunnel that silently exposes per-path operational data off-host.
275		t.reloadMu.Lock()
276		err = t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen)
277		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
278		if err != nil {
279			// The tunnel is fully constructed and holds the amnezia guard, so transfer that
280			// ownership to it (ok=true) BEFORE tearing it down: t.Close releases the guard via
281			// releaseOnce, and suppressing the !ok defer avoids a double release. t.Close also
282			// stops the probe loop and closes the engine/TUN.
283			ok = true
284			t.Close()
285			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: start metrics endpoint: %w", err)
286		}
287	
288		ok = true
289		clg.Info("tunnel up", "interface", name, "role", string(cfg.Role))
290		return t, nil
291	}
292	
293	// applyMetricsLocked reconciles the running /metrics endpoint to listen: it starts the
294	// endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty, and rebinds
295	// (stop old, start new) when the address changed. It is idempotent for an unchanged
296	// address. The Source is reused across a rebind so its derived-throughput state is not
297	// reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload both do), which also serializes it
298	// against Close reading metricsSrv. On a NewServer/refuse error the previous server is
299	// left running untouched, so a bad reload never drops a working endpoint.
300	func (t *Tunnel) applyMetricsLocked(listen string) error {
301		if listen == t.metricsListen {
302			return nil
303		}
304		if listen == "" {
305			t.stopMetricsLocked()
306			return nil
307		}
308		srv, err := metrics.NewServer(listen, t.metricsSrc, t.log)
309		if err != nil {
310			return err
311		}
312		t.stopMetricsLocked()
313		srv.Start()
314		t.metricsSrv = srv
315		t.metricsListen = listen
316		return nil
317	}
318	
319	// stopMetricsLocked gracefully shuts the running endpoint down (if any) and clears the
320	// bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu.
321	func (t *Tunnel) stopMetricsLocked() {
322		if t.metricsSrv == nil {
323			return
324		}
325		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), metricsShutdownTimeout)
326		defer cancel()
327		if err := t.metricsSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
328			t.log.Warn("metrics endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
329		}
330		t.metricsSrv = nil
331		t.metricsListen = ""
332	}
333	
334	// Reload applies a reloaded configuration to the RUNNING tunnel by diffing its
335	// path set against the live one and adding/removing paths at runtime (T30), WITHOUT
336	// tearing the tunnel down: the WG session, the surviving paths, and in-flight
337	// resequencing are all preserved. cfg is assumed already validated (config.Load
338	// fails fast on a bad file, so a bad reload never reaches here and never disturbs
339	// the running tunnel). Only the path set is diffed — the crypto/amnezia/role fields
340	// are fixed for the life of the process and a reload does not re-key the engine.
341	//
342	// New paths are added BEFORE absent ones are removed, so a failed addition aborts
343	// with every existing path still in service. Diffing is by name: a path present in
344	// both sets is left untouched (its source/dest parameters are not re-read).
345	//
346	// A membership-only reload CANNOT apply every kind of change: a same-name path whose
347	// source/dest changed, a path REORDER (index 0 is the preferred primary, so a reorder
348	// is a priority change), and every non-path field (psk, wireguard, amnezia, role, log,
349	// metrics) are all out of scope for T30. Rather than silently diverge from the file,
350	// Reload logs an EXPLICIT WARNING per ignored change (reloadWarnings) so the operator
351	// knows exactly what was dropped; only the path-membership add/remove is applied.
352	func (t *Tunnel) Reload(cfg *config.Config) error {
353		t.reloadMu.Lock()
354		defer t.reloadMu.Unlock()
355	
356		for _, w := range reloadWarnings(t.cfg, cfg) {
357			t.log.Warn("reload: ignored config change (path-membership reload only)", "change", w)
358		}
359	
360		// Rebind the /metrics endpoint if its listen address changed (T23). This is one of
361		// the few non-path fields a reload DOES apply: the endpoint is external to the WG
362		// session and the Bind, so restarting it disturbs neither. applyMetricsLocked reuses
363		// the tunnel's stable Source (its derived-throughput state survives), and on a refuse
364		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address) leaves the previous endpoint running and fails
365		// the reload — the running tunnel is never disturbed. Applied BEFORE the path diff so
366		// a metrics-refuse aborts before any membership change.
367		if cfg.Metrics.Listen != t.metricsListen {
368			if err := t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen); err != nil {
369				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload metrics endpoint: %w", err)
370			}
371			t.log.Info("reload: metrics endpoint rebound", "listen", cfg.Metrics.Listen)
372		}
373	
374		add, remove := diffPaths(t.bind.PathNames(), cfg.Paths)
375		for _, def := range add {
376			if err := t.bind.AddPath(def); err != nil {
377				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload add path %q: %w", def.Name, err)
378			}
379			t.log.Info("reload: path added", "path", def.Name)
380		}
381		for _, name := range remove {
382			if err := t.bind.RemovePath(name); err != nil {
383				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload remove path %q: %w", name, err)
384			}
385			t.log.Info("reload: path removed", "path", name)
386		}
387		// Advance the running config to the membership now in service. Survivors keep their
388		// ORIGINAL parameters and all non-path fields stay as booted (the ignored changes
389		// were not applied), so a subsequent identical reload re-warns about a still-diverged
390		// file rather than silently accepting it. Metrics.Listen is carried to the applied
391		// value so a subsequent reload diffs against the endpoint actually running.
392		t.cfg = runningConfig(t.cfg, add, remove)
393		t.cfg.Metrics.Listen = t.metricsListen
394		return nil
395	}
396	
397	// reloadWarnings compares the desired config against the currently-running one and
398	// returns one human-readable warning per change a path-membership-only reload (T30)
399	// cannot apply: a modified same-name path (source/dest), a path reorder, and any
400	// non-path field. Membership add/remove is NOT reported here — it is applied. Full
401	// modify-support is out of scope; SILENCE is not — the operator must be told what was
402	// ignored. It is a pure function so the warning set is unit-testable.
403	func reloadWarnings(live, desired *config.Config) []string {
404		var w []string
405	
406		if live.Role != desired.Role {
407			w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("role %q -> %q", live.Role, desired.Role))
408		}
409		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.PSK, desired.PSK) {
410			w = append(w, "psk changed")
411		}
412		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.WireGuard, desired.WireGuard) {
413			w = append(w, "wireguard section changed")
414		}
415		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Amnezia, desired.Amnezia) {
416			w = append(w, "amnezia section changed")
417		}
418		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Log, desired.Log) {
419			w = append(w, "log section changed")
420		}
421		// NOTE: a Metrics change is NOT warned here — unlike the other non-path fields, the
422		// reload APPLIES it by rebinding the /metrics endpoint (see Reload). Warning about a
423		// change that is honoured would misinform the operator.
424	
425		// Same-name paths whose parameters changed: diffPaths matches by name only, so a
426		// modified source/dest on an existing path is otherwise silently dropped.
427		liveByName := make(map[string]config.Path, len(live.Paths))
428		for _, p := range live.Paths {
429			liveByName[p.Name] = p
430		}
431		for _, d := range desired.Paths {
432			l, ok := liveByName[d.Name]
433			if !ok {
434				continue // a genuinely new path is added, not ignored
435			}
436			if l.SourceAddr != d.SourceAddr || l.DestAddr != d.DestAddr {
437				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q source/dest changed — the running path keeps its original binding", d.Name))
438			}
439		}
440	
441		if reordered(live.Paths, desired.Paths) {
442			w = append(w, "path priority order changed (reorder) — the running order is unchanged")
443		}
444		return w
445	}
446	
447	// reordered reports whether the paths common to BOTH slices appear in a different
448	// relative order in desired than in live. The common subsequence has the same length
449	// on both sides (it is the name intersection), so a positional mismatch is a reorder.
450	func reordered(live, desired []config.Path) bool {
451		desiredNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
452		for _, p := range desired {
453			desiredNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
454		}
455		liveNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
456		for _, p := range live {
457			liveNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
458		}
459		var liveCommon, desiredCommon []string
460		for _, p := range live {
461			if _, ok := desiredNames[p.Name]; ok {
462				liveCommon = append(liveCommon, p.Name)
463			}
464		}
465		for _, p := range desired {
466			if _, ok := liveNames[p.Name]; ok {
467				desiredCommon = append(desiredCommon, p.Name)
468			}
469		}
470		for i := range liveCommon {
471			if liveCommon[i] != desiredCommon[i] {
472				return true
473			}
474		}
475		return false
476	}
477	
478	// runningConfig advances the running config to the membership the bind now serves:
479	// the survivors (live paths not removed, keeping their ORIGINAL parameters and order)
480	// followed by the added paths (their new parameters). Non-path fields are carried over
481	// unchanged — a membership-only reload does not re-apply them. It mirrors the m.defs
482	// order the bind rebuilds on a Close→Open (survivors-in-order, then appended adds).
483	func runningConfig(live *config.Config, add []config.Path, remove []string) *config.Config {
484		removeSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(remove))
485		for _, n := range remove {
486			removeSet[n] = struct{}{}
487		}
488		paths := make([]config.Path, 0, len(live.Paths)+len(add))
489		for _, p := range live.Paths {
490			if _, gone := removeSet[p.Name]; !gone {
491				paths = append(paths, p)
492			}
493		}
494		paths = append(paths, add...)
495		running := *live
496		running.Paths = paths
497		return &running
498	}
499	
500	// diffPaths computes, by name, which desired paths are not yet live (to add) and
501	// which live paths are no longer desired (to remove). It is a pure function so the
502	// reload diff is unit-testable without a running engine.
503	func diffPaths(live []string, desired []config.Path) (add []config.Path, remove []string) {
504		liveSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
505		for _, n := range live {
506			liveSet[n] = struct{}{}
507		}
508		desiredSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
509		for _, p := range desired {
510			desiredSet[p.Name] = struct{}{}
511			if _, ok := liveSet[p.Name]; !ok {
512				add = append(add, p)
513			}
514		}
515		for _, n := range live {
516			if _, ok := desiredSet[n]; !ok {
517				remove = append(remove, n)
518			}
519		}
520		return add, remove
521	}
522	
523	// defaultFailbackDwell is how long a recovered higher-priority path must stay up
524	// before egress fails BACK to it, damping flap-induced thrash (T15 hysteresis).
525	// Unlike the probe-cadence/liveness thresholds (which are the shared
526	// telemetry.Default* single source of truth, D16), the failback dwell is not part
527	// of the failover-recovery budget — failover to a backup is instant — so it stays a
528	// device-local constant.
529	const defaultFailbackDwell = 5 * time.Second
530	
531	// fecConfig maps the validated [fec] config block onto the fec.Config the multipath
532	// Bind consumes (T24), or returns nil when FEC is disabled so the Bind runs the
533	// datapath with no parity plane. The ratio was already range-checked in config
534	// validation; the Bind re-validates defensively.
535	func fecConfig(f config.FEC) *fec.Config {
536		if !f.Enabled {
537			return nil
538		}
539		return &fec.Config{
540			DataShards:   f.DataShards,
541			ParityShards: f.ParityShards,
542			Deadline:     f.Deadline,
543		}
544	}
545	
546	// adaptiveFECConfig maps the [fec] block onto the adaptive controller config the multipath
547	// Bind drives (T29), or returns nil when FEC is disabled or the block did not opt into
548	// adaptive mode — in which case the Bind runs the fixed-ratio plane (T24) unchanged. The
549	// controller uses the simulation-proven default control law (internal/adaptivefec,
550	// DefaultConfig), with only the group geometry pinned to the configured ratio: DataShards
551	// (K) is the FEC data_shards and MaxParity (the parity CEILING) is the FEC parity_shards,
552	// which the receiver's decoder is built at. The Bind re-validates and cross-checks these
553	// against the FEC config defensively.
554	func adaptiveFECConfig(f config.FEC) *adaptivefec.Config {
555		if !f.Enabled || !f.Adaptive {
556			return nil
557		}
558		c := adaptivefec.DefaultConfig()
559		c.DataShards = f.DataShards
560		c.MaxParity = f.ParityShards
561		// Exactly one sizing mode is active (config load enforces mutual exclusion): in the
562		// residual-SLA mode f.SafetyFactor is 0 and f.TargetResidual drives M; in the legacy
563		// mode f.TargetResidual is 0 and f.SafetyFactor (defaulted at load) drives M.
564		c.SafetyFactor = f.SafetyFactor
565		c.TargetResidual = f.TargetResidual
566		return &c
567	}
568	
569	// buildScheduler constructs one live *telemetry.Prober per path and the P1
570	// active-backup send scheduler over them, in cfg.Paths' configured priority order
571	// (index 0 = the preferred primary). The returned probers ARE the scheduler's
572	// PathHealth sources (a *Prober is internally synchronized, satisfying the
573	// PathHealth concurrency contract — a bare *Liveness would not) and are handed to
574	// the bind so the probe transport drives the very same liveness the scheduler
575	// selects on. This replaces the T15 sched.AlwaysUp placeholder with real on-wire
576	// failover (T37).
577	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
578		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
579		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
580			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
581			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
582				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
583				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
584			},
585		}
586		// One random per-boot session id shared by every path's Prober (it identifies
587		// this boot, not the path): a peer restart presents a new session id that resets
588		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12).
589		// A runtime-added path (T30) reuses the SAME session id, so its probes join this
590		// boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a challenge reset.
591		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
592		if err != nil {
593			return nil, nil, nil, err
594		}
595		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock. It is
596		// the single construction point for boot-time AND runtime paths, so both measure
597		// liveness identically.
598		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
599			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, cfg.PSK, proberCfg, clock, lg)
600		}
601		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
602		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
603		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
604		for i := range cfg.Paths {
605			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
606			health[i] = probers[i]
607			quality[i] = probers[i]
608		}
609		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
610		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
611		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
612		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
613		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
614		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
615		if err != nil {
616			return nil, nil, nil, err
617		}
618		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
619	}
620	
621	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
622	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
623	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
624	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
625	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.
626	func selectScheduler(cfg *config.Config, health []sched.PathHealth, quality []sched.PathQuality, clock telemetry.Clock, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, error) {
627		switch cfg.Scheduler.Policy {
628		case config.PolicyWeighted:
629			sc := cfg.Scheduler
630			return sched.NewWeighted(health, quality, sched.WeightedConfig{
631				PerPathCapacity:   sc.PerPathCapacityFPS,
632				EngageFraction:    sc.EngageFraction,
633				DisengageFraction: sc.DisengageFraction,
634				CollapseDwell:     sc.CollapseDwell,
635				LoadTau:           sc.LoadTau,
636				Pacing:            sc.PacingEnabled,
637				PacingBurst:       sc.PacingBurstFrames,
638				WeightRTTFloor:    sc.WeightRTTFloor,
639				WeightLossFloor:   sc.WeightLossFloor,
640			}, clock, lg)
641		default:
642			// active-backup (and the empty default, normalized to it at config load).
643			return sched.NewActiveBackup(
644				health,
645				sched.Config{FailbackAfter: defaultFailbackDwell},
646				clock,
647				lg,
648			)
649		}
650	}
651	
652	// Name is the created TUN interface name (for external addressing/routing).
653	func (t *Tunnel) Name() string { return t.name }
654	
655	// Wait blocks until the device is torn down (its own Close, or an unrecoverable
656	// engine error).
657	func (t *Tunnel) Wait() { <-t.dev.Wait() }
658	
659	// Close stops the probe loop, brings the device down, and releases the TUN and
660	// Bind. Idempotent. It also releases this tunnel's hold on the single-amnezia-
661	// engine-per-process guard exactly once, so a later Up may reconfigure the
662	// process-global amnezia state. Probing is stopped BEFORE the engine tears the
663	// bind's sockets down so no emission races the close.
664	func (t *Tunnel) Close() {
665		// Shut the scrape endpoint FIRST so no in-flight /metrics scrape reads the Bind while
666		// the engine tears its sockets down. reloadMu serializes this against a concurrent
667		// SIGHUP-driven rebind.
668		t.reloadMu.Lock()
669		t.stopMetricsLocked()
670		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
671		if t.stopProbes != nil {
672			t.stopProbes()
673		}
674		if t.stopReconcile != nil {
675			t.stopReconcile()
676		}
677		if t.stopHubFailover != nil {
678			t.stopHubFailover()
679		}
680		t.dev.Close()
681		t.releaseOnce.Do(func() { globalAmneziaGuard.release(t.amnezia) })
682	}
683	
684	// engineLogger adapts the amneziawg engine's logger onto wanbond's structured
685	// logger under a "wg" component. The engine is verbose only when the daemon runs
686	// at debug level; otherwise only its errors surface.
687	func engineLogger(lg log.Logger, level string) *awgdevice.Logger {
688		wg := lg.Component("wg")
689		verbosef := func(string, ...any) {}
690		if strings.EqualFold(strings.TrimSpace(level), "debug") {
691			verbosef = func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Debug(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) }
692		}
693		return &awgdevice.Logger{
694			Verbosef: verbosef,
695			Errorf:   func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Error(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) },
696		}
697	}
698	
699	// uapiConfig renders cfg into the newline-delimited UAPI set string the engine's
700	// IpcSet consumes. Keys are lowercase hex (UAPI's on-the-wire encoding), NOT the
701	// base64 form the TOML carries. Amnezia obfuscation keys are emitted only when the
702	// block is configured; an all-zero Amnezia block leaves the engine in plain
703	// WireGuard mode. The same amnezia parameters are applied on BOTH roles (edge and
704	// concentrator) as defense-in-depth — they must match end to end for the handshake
705	// to succeed (config validation makes each end specify a complete profile, D1).
706	func uapiConfig(cfg *config.Config) (string, error) {
707		var b strings.Builder
708	
709		priv := cfg.WireGuard.PrivateKey.Bytes()
710		fmt.Fprintf(&b, "private_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(priv[:]))
711		if cfg.Role == config.RoleConcentrator {
712			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "listen_port=%d\n", cfg.WireGuard.ListenPort)
713		}
714		writeAmnezia(&b, cfg.Amnezia)
715	
716		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
717			pub := peer.PublicKey.Bytes()
718			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(pub[:]))
719			if len(peer.Endpoints) > 0 {
720				// Endpoints[0] is the active/primary concentrator (Q18 ordered-endpoint
721				// active-standby); it is populated from either the legacy single
722				// `endpoint` field or the new ordered `endpoints` list (config.Peer.
723				// resolveEndpoints), so both forms bring up the tunnel identically here.
724				// Switching to a standby endpoint on hub loss is T57's job, not this
725				// initial UAPI render.
726				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "endpoint=%s\n", peer.Endpoints[0])
727				// A keepalive keeps the edge->concentrator session warm and lets the
728				// concentrator relearn the edge endpoint after a NAT rebind; only the
729				// initiating (edge) side sets it.
730				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "persistent_keepalive_interval=%d\n", keepaliveSeconds)
731			}
732			if len(peer.AllowedIPs) == 0 {
733				return "", fmt.Errorf("peer %d (%s): at least one allowed_ip is required", i, hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8]))
734			}
735			for _, cidr := range peer.AllowedIPs {
736				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "allowed_ip=%s\n", cidr)
737			}
738		}
739		return b.String(), nil
740	}
741	
742	// keepaliveSeconds is the edge's persistent-keepalive interval.
743	const keepaliveSeconds = 25
744	
745	// writeAmnezia emits the nine amneziawg obfuscation UAPI keys, but only when the
746	// block is configured. When amnezia is unused, wanbond leaves the engine's
747	// PROCESS-GLOBAL message-type state untouched (see the amneziaGuard invariant).
748	// Config validation guarantees a configured block is complete and self-consistent
749	// (D1), and applyDefaults has already filled the standard magic headers (1..4)
750	// when they were omitted, so the emitted set never carries an h*=0 sentinel.
751	func writeAmnezia(b *strings.Builder, a config.Amnezia) {
752		if !a.Configured() {
753			return
754		}
755		fmt.Fprintf(b, "jc=%d\njmin=%d\njmax=%d\ns1=%d\ns2=%d\nh1=%d\nh2=%d\nh3=%d\nh4=%d\n",
756			a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2, a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
757	}

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"math"
8		"net/netip"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11		"time"
12	
13		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
14	)
15	
16	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
17	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
18	type Role string
19	
20	const (
21		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
22		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
23		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
24		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
25	)
26	
27	func (r Role) valid() bool {
28		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
29	}
30	
31	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
32	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
33	const keyLen = 32
34	
35	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
36	// from a single TOML file.
37	type Config struct {
38		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
39		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
40		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
41		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
42		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
43		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
44		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
45		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
46		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
47	}
48	
49	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
50	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
51	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
52	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
53	type SchedulerPolicy string
54	
55	const (
56		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
57		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
58		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
59		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
60		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
61		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
62		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
63		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
64		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
65	)
66	
67	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
68		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
69	}
70	
71	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
72	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
73	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
74	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
75	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
76	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
77	const (
78		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
79		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
80		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
81		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
82		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
83		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
84		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
85		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
86		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
87		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
88		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
89		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
90		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
91		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
92		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
93		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
94		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
95		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
96		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
97		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
98		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
99		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
100		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
101		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
102		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
103		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
104		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
105		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
106		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
107		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
108		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
109		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
110		// alone rather than both diverging.
111		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
112	)
113	
114	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
115	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
116	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
117	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
118	type SchedulerConfig struct {
119		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
120		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
121	
122		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
123		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
124		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
125		// under the weighted policy.
126		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
127		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
128		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
129		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
130		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
131		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
132		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
133		// band.
134		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
135		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
136		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
137		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
138		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
139		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`
140	
141		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
142		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
143		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
144		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
145		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
146		// when PacingEnabled.
147		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
148	
149		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
150		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
151		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
152		// weighted).
153		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
154	}
155	
156	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
157	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
158	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
159	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
160	type BDPSizing struct {
161		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
162		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
163		CapacityFPS float64
164		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
165		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
166		// jitter without building a standing queue.
167		BurstFrames float64
168	}
169	
170	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
171	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
172	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
173	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
174	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
175	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
176	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
177	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
178	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
179	
180	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
181	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
182	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
183	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
184	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
185	//
186	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
187	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
188	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
189	//
190	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
191	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
192	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
193	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
194	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
195		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
196			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
197		}
198		if rtt <= 0 {
199			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
200		}
201		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
202			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
203		}
204		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
205		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
206		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
207		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
208	}
209	
210	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
211	type bandwidthUnit struct {
212		suffix string
213		mult   float64
214	}
215	
216	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
217	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
218	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
219	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
220		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
221		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
222		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
223		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
224	}
225	
226	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
227	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
228	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
229	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
230	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
231		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
232		if lower == "" {
233			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
234		}
235		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
236			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
237				continue
238			}
239			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
240			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
241			if err != nil {
242				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
243			}
244			return val * u.mult, nil
245		}
246		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
247	}
248	
249	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
250	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
251	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
252	// it until the size threshold fills.
253	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
254	
255	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
256	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
257	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
258	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
259	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
260	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
261	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
262	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
263	
264	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
265	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
266	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
267	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
268	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
269	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
270	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
271	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
272	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
273	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
274	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
275	
276	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
277	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It
278	// is restated here so config load fails fast on an over-large ratio at the right
279	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.
280	const maxFECShards = 256
281	
282	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
283	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
284	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
285	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
286	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
287	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
288	type FEC struct {
289		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
290		// datapath carries no parity.
291		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
292		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
293		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
294		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
295		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
296		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
297		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
298		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
299		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
300		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
301		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
302		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
303		// > 0 after defaulting.
304		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
305		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
306		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
307		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
308		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
309		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
310		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
311		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
312		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
313		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
314		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
315		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
316		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
317		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
318		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
319		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
320		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
321		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
322		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
323		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
324		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
325		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
326		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
327		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
328		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
329		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
330		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
331		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
332		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
333	}
334	
335	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
336	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
337	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.
338	func (f *FEC) applyDefaults() {
339		if !f.Enabled {
340			return
341		}
342		if f.Deadline == 0 {
343			f.Deadline = defaultFECDeadline
344		}
345		// The SafetyFactor default is applied only in the legacy sizing mode: when
346		// target_residual is set it is the primary surface and safety_factor stays inert
347		// (0), so defaulting it would spuriously trip the mutual-exclusion check.
348		if f.Adaptive && f.TargetResidual == 0 && f.SafetyFactor == 0 {
349			f.SafetyFactor = defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor
350		}
351	}
352	
353	// validate enforces the FEC ratio invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned parity
354	// ratio is rejected at load rather than panicking the codec at runtime. A disabled
355	// block needs no tuning. The bounds mirror internal/fec's own Config.validate.
356	func (f FEC) validate() error {
357		if !f.Enabled {
358			if f.Adaptive {
359				return errors.New("fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true (adaptive FEC is meaningless with the plane off)")
360			}
361			return nil
362		}
363		if f.DataShards < 1 {
364			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.DataShards)
365		}
366		if f.ParityShards < 1 {
367			return fmt.Errorf("fec.parity_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.ParityShards)
368		}
369		if f.DataShards+f.ParityShards > maxFECShards {
370			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards + fec.parity_shards must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", maxFECShards, f.DataShards+f.ParityShards)
371		}
372		if f.Deadline <= 0 {
373			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be > 0 when FEC is enabled, got %s", f.Deadline)
374		}
375		if f.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
376			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be <= %s (safely below the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped), got %s", maxFECDeadline, f.Deadline)
377		}
378		if f.Adaptive {
379			if f.TargetResidual != 0 {
380				// Residual-SLA sizing mode (primary): target_residual governs and safety_factor
381				// stays inert, so setting both is an ambiguous mis-config — reject it. NaN is
382				// caught explicitly because every ordered comparison against it is false.
383				if math.IsNaN(f.TargetResidual) || f.TargetResidual <= 0 || f.TargetResidual >= 1 {
384					return fmt.Errorf("fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1), got %g", f.TargetResidual)
385				}
386				if f.SafetyFactor != 0 {
387					return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor and fec.target_residual are mutually exclusive; target_residual is the primary residual-SLA surface — set one, not both")
388				}
389			} else if f.SafetyFactor < 1 {
390				// SafetyFactor is defaulted to defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor when zero, so a value
391				// below 1 here is an explicit mis-set: the controller requires >= 1 (masking less
392				// than the mean loss is nonsensical), matching adaptivefec.Config.Validate.
393				return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor must be >= 1 in adaptive mode, got %g", f.SafetyFactor)
394			}
395		} else if f.SafetyFactor != 0 || f.TargetResidual != 0 {
396			return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor / fec.target_residual are only meaningful in adaptive mode (set fec.adaptive = true), got safety_factor=%g target_residual=%g", f.SafetyFactor, f.TargetResidual)
397		}
398		return nil
399	}

401	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
402	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
403	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
404	type Path struct {
405		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
406		Name string `toml:"name"`
407		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
408		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
409		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
410		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
411		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
412		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
413		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
414		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
415		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
416		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
417		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
418		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
419		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
420		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
421		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
422		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
423		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
424		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
425		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
426		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
427		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
428		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
429		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
430		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
431		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
432		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
433		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
434		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
435		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
436		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
437		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
438		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`
439	}

441	// WireGuard holds the inner tunnel's key material.
442	type WireGuard struct {
443		PrivateKey Key    `toml:"private_key"`
444		Peers      []Peer `toml:"peers"`
445		// ListenPort is the UDP port the concentrator listens on; 0 on the edge.
446		ListenPort uint16 `toml:"listen_port"`
447	}
448	
449	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
450	type Peer struct {
451		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
452		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
453		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
454		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
455		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
456		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
457		// config surface.
458		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
459		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
460		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
461		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
462		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
463		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
464		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
465		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
466		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
467		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
468		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
469		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
470		// active one are down.
471		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
472		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
473		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
474	}
475	
476	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
477	// Endpoints list (Q18): the legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered
478	// EndpointsRaw list are mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint =
479	// "..."` is normalized here to a ONE-ELEMENT Endpoints list — so it stays
480	// behavior-identical to the pre-T54 single-defaultRemote config surface. Each
481	// entry must parse as netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format
482	// bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint requires of the UAPI endpoint string at
483	// runtime, and duplicates within the list are rejected.
484	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
485		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
486			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
487		}
488		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
489		if p.Endpoint != "" {
490			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
491		}
492		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
493		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
494		for _, s := range raw {
495			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
496			if err != nil {
497				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
498			}
499			if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
500				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
501			}
502			seen[ap] = struct{}{}
503			endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
504		}
505		p.Endpoints = endpoints
506		return nil
507	}

509	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
510	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
511	//
512	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
513	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
514	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
515	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
516	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
517	type Amnezia struct {
518		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
519		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
520		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
521		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
522		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
523		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
524		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
525		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
526		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
527	}
528	
529	// defaultMagicHeaders are the standard WireGuard message-type headers: initiation,
530	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
531	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
532	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
533	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
534	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
535	
536	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
537	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
538	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
539		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
540			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
541	}
542	
543	// applyDefaults fills in the standard magic headers (1..4) when the block is
544	// configured but no header was given, so the UAPI renderer emits an explicit,
545	// complete header set instead of h1=0..h4=0. It is a no-op for an unconfigured
546	// block and for one that already sets any header (a partial header set is left
547	// intact so validate can reject it).
548	func (a *Amnezia) applyDefaults() {
549		if !a.Configured() {
550			return
551		}
552		if a.H1 == 0 && a.H2 == 0 && a.H3 == 0 && a.H4 == 0 {
553			a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4 = defaultMagicHeaders[0], defaultMagicHeaders[1], defaultMagicHeaders[2], defaultMagicHeaders[3]
554		}
555	}

568	// Key is a 32-byte Curve25519 key or PSK, carried in TOML as standard base64.
569	type Key struct {
570		bytes [keyLen]byte
571		set   bool
572	}
573	
574	// UnmarshalText decodes a base64 key. An empty string leaves the Key unset so
575	// optional keys can be distinguished from present-but-invalid ones.
576	func (k *Key) UnmarshalText(text []byte) error {
577		if len(text) == 0 {
578			return nil
579		}
580		raw, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(string(text))
581		if err != nil {
582			return fmt.Errorf("key is not valid base64: %w", err)
583		}
584		if len(raw) != keyLen {
585			return fmt.Errorf("key must decode to %d bytes, got %d", keyLen, len(raw))
586		}
587		copy(k.bytes[:], raw)
588		k.set = true
589		return nil
590	}
591	
592	// IsSet reports whether the key was present and valid.
593	func (k Key) IsSet() bool { return k.set }
594	
595	// Bytes returns the raw key material.
596	func (k Key) Bytes() [keyLen]byte { return k.bytes }
